steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Words in Context question. The passage sets up a conditional: average ratings can be misleading with few raters, but Food Chain Magnate has over 9,000 raters. The blank must describe what 9,000 raters makes the sample—the decisive clue is that 9,000 is enough to “feel confident” the rating is reliable.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘insufficient’: means not enough. This contradicts the passage, which uses 9,000 raters to justify confidence in the average. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘excessive’: means more than necessary. The passage makes no claim that 9,000 is too many; it simply says 9,000 is enough to be reliable. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘accurate’: describes a quality of the rating itself, not of the sample size. A number of raters cannot be “accurate”—accuracy is not the relevant attribute for a count. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘adequate’: means sufficient for a purpose. 9,000 raters is adequate—just enough—to feel confident the average rating is reliable. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
